Abstract Vision backbone networks play a central role in modern computer vision. Enhancing their efficiency directly benefits a wide range of downstream applications. To measure efficiency, many publications rely on MACs (Multiply Accumulate operations) as a predictor of execution time. In this paper, we experimentally demonstrate the shortcomings of such a metric, especially in the context of edge devices. By contrasting the MAC count and execution time of common architectural design elements, we identify key factors for efficient execution and provide insights to optimize backbone design. Based on these insights, we present LowFormer, a novel vision backbone family. LowFormer features a streamlined macro and micro design that includes Lowtention, a lightweight alternative to Multi-Head Self-Attention. Lowtention not only proves more efficient, but also enables superior results on ImageNet. Additionally, we present an edge GPU version of LowFormer, that can further improve upon its baseline’s speed on edge GPU and desktop GPU. We demonstrate the LowFormer’s wide applicability by evaluating it on smaller image classification datasets, as well as adapting it to several downstream tasks, such as object detection, semantic segmentation, image retrieval, and visual object tracking. LowFormer models consistently achieve remarkable speed-ups across various hardware platforms compared to recent state-of-the-art backbones. Code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/altair199797/LowFormer.

item.title: Physics-Guided Stokes-Consistent Fusion of DoLP–AoP Representations for Polarization Image Restoration
item.dc:creator: Sihai Qiao
Rong Chen
Hui Li
Liying Hao
item.description: Polarization imaging provides multiple complementary representations of the same scene, including raw polarization views, Stokes parameters, and polarization descriptors such as DoLP and AoP. However, due to the nonlinear transformations involved in Stokes-based computation, noise in the intensity domain can be significantly amplified, leading to inconsistent and unreliable polarization representations. This makes effective fusion across representations particularly challenging. In this work, we formulate polarization image restoration as a physics-guided multi-representation fusion problem and propose a Stokes-consistent fusion framework. Instead of treating different polarization representations independently, the proposed method explicitly enforces Stokes-consistent constraints to couple S 0 , DoLP, and AoP within a unified reconstruction process. This design ensures cross-representation consistency and reduces error propagation under heterogeneous noise conditions. To support this framework, we employ a cascaded residual dense fusion module to aggregate reliable local polarization cues, and a top-activated sparse transformer to capture long-range dependencies for globally consistent reconstruction. To evaluate robustness and generalization, we construct a polarization dataset with controlled exposure variations and conduct extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world data. The results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (SoTA) approaches, achieving improved restoration quality and more reliable polarization representation under diverse noise conditions. Published: 2026-05-28T07:08:06+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.790 (must_read)

Authors: Sihai Qiao; Rong Chen; Hui Li; Liying Hao

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104503

Relevance: 0.790 (must_read)

Polarization imaging provides multiple complementary representations of the same scene, including raw polarization views, Stokes parameters, and polarization descriptors such as DoLP and AoP. However, due to the nonlinear transformations involved in Stokes-based computation, noise in the intensity domain can be significantly amplified, leading to inconsistent and unreliable polarization representations. This makes effective fusion across representations particularly challenging. In this work, we formulate polarization image restoration as a physics-guided multi-representation fusion problem and propose a Stokes-consistent fusion framework. Instead of treating different polarization representations independently, the proposed method explicitly enforces Stokes-consistent constraints to couple S 0 , DoLP, and AoP within a unified reconstruction process. This design ensures cross-representation consistency and reduces error propagation under heterogeneous noise conditions. To support this framework, we employ a cascaded residual dense fusion module to aggregate reliable local polarization cues, and a top-activated sparse transformer to capture long-range dependencies for globally consistent reconstruction. To evaluate robustness and generalization, we construct a polarization dataset with controlled exposure variations and conduct extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world data. The results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (SoTA) approaches, achieving improved restoration quality and more reliable polarization representation under diverse noise conditions.

item.title: SD-ReID: View-aware Stable Diffusion for Aerial-Ground Person Re-Identification
item.dc:creator: Yuhao Wang
Xiang Hu
Lixin Wang
Pingping Zhang
Huchuan Lu
item.description: Aerial-Ground Person Re-IDentification (AG-ReID) aims to retrieve specific persons across cameras with different viewpoints. Previous works focus on designing discriminative models to maintain the identity consistency despite drastic changes in camera viewpoints. The core idea behind these methods is quite natural, but designing a view-robust model is a very challenging task. Moreover, they overlook the contribution of view-specific features in enhancing the model’s ability to represent persons. To address these issues, we propose a novel generative framework named SD-ReID for AG-ReID, which leverages generative models to mimic the feature distribution of different views while extracting robust identity representations. More specifically, we first train a ViT-based model to extract person representations along with controllable conditions, including identity and view conditions. We then fine-tune the Stable Diffusion (SD) model to enhance person representations guided by these controllable conditions. Furthermore, we introduce the View-Refined Decoder (VRD) to bridge the gap between instance-level and global-level features. Finally, both person representations and all-view features are employed to retrieve target persons. Extensive experiments on five AG-ReID benchmarks (i.e., CARGO, AG-ReIDv1, AG-ReIDv2, LAGPeR and G2APS-ReID) demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method. The source code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/924973292/SD-ReID. Published: 2026-05-28T22:56:47+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.790 (must_read)

Aerial-Ground Person Re-IDentification (AG-ReID) aims to retrieve specific persons across cameras with different viewpoints. Previous works focus on designing discriminative models to maintain the identity consistency despite drastic changes in camera viewpoints. The core idea behind these methods is quite natural, but designing a view-robust model is a very challenging task. Moreover, they overlook the contribution of view-specific features in enhancing the model’s ability to represent persons. To address these issues, we propose a novel generative framework named SD-ReID for AG-ReID, which leverages generative models to mimic the feature distribution of different views while extracting robust identity representations. More specifically, we first train a ViT-based model to extract person representations along with controllable conditions, including identity and view conditions. We then fine-tune the Stable Diffusion (SD) model to enhance person representations guided by these controllable conditions. Furthermore, we introduce the View-Refined Decoder (VRD) to bridge the gap between instance-level and global-level features. Finally, both person representations and all-view features are employed to retrieve target persons. Extensive experiments on five AG-ReID benchmarks (i.e., CARGO, AG-ReIDv1, AG-ReIDv2, LAGPeR and G2APS-ReID) demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method. The source code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/924973292/SD-ReID.

item.title: Decouple-Then-Synergize: A Self-Paced Collaborative Learning Network for RGB-T Snowy Urban Scene Parsing
item.dc:creator: Wujie Zhou
Yiben Li
Qiuping Jiang
Liang Liao
Runmin Cong
Weisi Lin
item.description: Fusing RGB and thermal infrared images is essential for advancing urban scene analysis. However, both modalities exhibit severe performance degradation under snowy conditions. Although independent enhancement modules can partially mitigate this issue, stacking multiple modules with different functions increases model complexity and may cause intermodular interference. To address these limitations, we propose a “decouple-then-synergize” framework that decouples the task into frequency-oriented enhancement and spatial semantic fusion, implemented by FRENet (frequency restoration enhancement network) and SIFNet (spatial interactive fusion network), respectively. FRENet uses an asymmetric enhancement strategy that selectively sharpens RGB color gradients while amplifying faint thermal targets. It incorporates a precise spectral refinement module to restore high-frequency details. SIFNet introduces a Mamba zipper fusion module to achieve robust interaction of high-level semantics and performs a reconstruction task to implicitly integrate thermal features into the RGB stream. To ensure effective collaboration between the two networks, we design a self-paced curriculum that manages bidirectional knowledge exchange at both the sample and pixel levels. This approach enables the networks to evolve into their enhanced versions, namely FRENet-collaborative learning (CL) and SIFNet-CL. Extensive experiments on the SUS and PST900 datasets demonstrate that our framework outperforms state-of-the-art scene parsing methods. The code and associated results are available at https://github.com/Lyb-2001/SPCL. Published: 2026-05-29T19:58:30+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing Score: 0.788 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Wujie Zhou; Yiben Li; Qiuping Jiang; Liang Liao; Runmin Cong; Weisi Lin

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing

DOI: 10.1109/tip.2026.3696109

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

Fusing RGB and thermal infrared images is essential for advancing urban scene analysis. However, both modalities exhibit severe performance degradation under snowy conditions. Although independent enhancement modules can partially mitigate this issue, stacking multiple modules with different functions increases model complexity and may cause intermodular interference. To address these limitations, we propose a “decouple-then-synergize” framework that decouples the task into frequency-oriented enhancement and spatial semantic fusion, implemented by FRENet (frequency restoration enhancement network) and SIFNet (spatial interactive fusion network), respectively. FRENet uses an asymmetric enhancement strategy that selectively sharpens RGB color gradients while amplifying faint thermal targets. It incorporates a precise spectral refinement module to restore high-frequency details. SIFNet introduces a Mamba zipper fusion module to achieve robust interaction of high-level semantics and performs a reconstruction task to implicitly integrate thermal features into the RGB stream. To ensure effective collaboration between the two networks, we design a self-paced curriculum that manages bidirectional knowledge exchange at both the sample and pixel levels. This approach enables the networks to evolve into their enhanced versions, namely FRENet-collaborative learning (CL) and SIFNet-CL. Extensive experiments on the SUS and PST900 datasets demonstrate that our framework outperforms state-of-the-art scene parsing methods. The code and associated results are available at https://github.com/Lyb-2001/SPCL.

item.title: TA-Diff: Topology-Aware Diffusion Networks for Joint 3D Hand Pose Estimation and Mesh Reconstruction
item.dc:creator: Wenji Yang
Xiangyu Que
item.description: Accurately recovering 3D hand pose and mesh from monocular 2D joint keypoints remains a fundamental yet challenging problem in computer vision. Although diffusion models have shown promising results in this field, existing methods often overlook hand anatomical structures and topological constraints, resulting in anatomically implausible pose predictions and limited mesh reconstruction accuracy. To address these limitations, we propose TA-Diff, a topology-aware diffusion framework that significantly enhances pose estimation precision and mesh reconstruction consistency by incorporating anatomical and topological constraints. Specifically, we design a hierarchical functional grouping encoding mechanism that partitions joints into semantically coherent functional groups to capture common features among homologous joints. We further introduce learnable hand topological structures that leverage anatomical priors to construct structured attention patterns, accurately modeling structural relationships between joints. To achieve high-quality reconstruction from joints to mesh, we further design a joint-guided mesh reconstruction module that efficiently maps joint semantics to vertex features through dual attention mechanisms, ensuring structural consistency between pose and mesh. Experiments on the FreiHAND and HO3Dv2 datasets demonstrate that TA-Diff achieves significant advantages compared to current state-of-the-art methods, reducing PA-MPJPE by 61.7% and 57.3% respectively, and PA-MPVPE by 8.8% and 6.5% respectively. Code and dataset are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TADiff-E16B/ . Published: 2026-05-28T07:08:13+00:00 Venue: Information Fusion Score: 0.788 (must_read)

Authors: Wenji Yang; Xiangyu Que

Journal: Information Fusion

DOI: 10.1016/j.inffus.2026.104504

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

Accurately recovering 3D hand pose and mesh from monocular 2D joint keypoints remains a fundamental yet challenging problem in computer vision. Although diffusion models have shown promising results in this field, existing methods often overlook hand anatomical structures and topological constraints, resulting in anatomically implausible pose predictions and limited mesh reconstruction accuracy. To address these limitations, we propose TA-Diff, a topology-aware diffusion framework that significantly enhances pose estimation precision and mesh reconstruction consistency by incorporating anatomical and topological constraints. Specifically, we design a hierarchical functional grouping encoding mechanism that partitions joints into semantically coherent functional groups to capture common features among homologous joints. We further introduce learnable hand topological structures that leverage anatomical priors to construct structured attention patterns, accurately modeling structural relationships between joints. To achieve high-quality reconstruction from joints to mesh, we further design a joint-guided mesh reconstruction module that efficiently maps joint semantics to vertex features through dual attention mechanisms, ensuring structural consistency between pose and mesh. Experiments on the FreiHAND and HO3Dv2 datasets demonstrate that TA-Diff achieves significant advantages compared to current state-of-the-art methods, reducing PA-MPJPE by 61.7% and 57.3% respectively, and PA-MPVPE by 8.8% and 6.5% respectively. Code and dataset are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TADiff-E16B/ .

item.title: GiPL: Generative augmented iterative Pseudo-Labeling for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Jiacong Liu
Shu Luo
Yikai Qin
Yaze Zhao
Yongwei Jiang
Yixiong Zou
item.description: Vision-language foundation models have shown promising zero-shot generalization for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection (CD-FSOD). However, they face two critical challenges in fine-tuning: insufficient support set utilization due to sparse single-instance annotations, and severe overfitting under extremely limited target-domain samples. To address these issues, this paper proposes GiPL, an efficient two-branch training framework.In the first branch, we design an iterative pseudo-label self-training paradigm, which performs zero-shot inference on the support set to generate reliable pseudo-annotations, fuses them with ground-truth labels, and iteratively optimizes the model to fully exploit support set data. In the second branch, we introduce generative data augmentation pipeline using large vision-language models, which synthesizes domain-aligned, multi-object annotated images to enrich training samples and suppress overfitting. Extensive experiments on three challenging CD-FSOD datasets (RUOD, CARPK, CarDD) under 1/5/10-shot settings demonstrate that GiPL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods with significant performance gains.Code is available at \href{https://github.com/z-yaz/CDiscover}{CDiscover}. Published: 2026-05-28T07:53:40+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.788 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jiacong Liu; Shu Luo; Yikai Qin; Yaze Zhao; Yongwei Jiang; Yixiong Zou

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.788 (must_read)

Vision-language foundation models have shown promising zero-shot generalization for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection (CD-FSOD). However, they face two critical challenges in fine-tuning: insufficient support set utilization due to sparse single-instance annotations, and severe overfitting under extremely limited target-domain samples. To address these issues, this paper proposes GiPL, an efficient two-branch training framework.In the first branch, we design an iterative pseudo-label self-training paradigm, which performs zero-shot inference on the support set to generate reliable pseudo-annotations, fuses them with ground-truth labels, and iteratively optimizes the model to fully exploit support set data. In the second branch, we introduce generative data augmentation pipeline using large vision-language models, which synthesizes domain-aligned, multi-object annotated images to enrich training samples and suppress overfitting. Extensive experiments on three challenging CD-FSOD datasets (RUOD, CARPK, CarDD) under 1/5/10-shot settings demonstrate that GiPL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods with significant performance gains.Code is available at \href{https://github.com/z-yaz/CDiscover}{CDiscover}.

item.title: Continual Test-Time Adaptation for Object Detection with Adaptive Monitoring and Randomized Restoration
item.dc:creator: Shilei Cao
Juepeng Zheng
Yan Liu
Baoquan Zhao
Ziqi Yuan
Weijia Li
Runmin Dong
Haohuan Fu
item.description: Real-world application models are commonly deployed in dynamic environments, where the target domain distribution undergoes temporal changes. Continual Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) has recently emerged as a promising technique to gradually adapt a source-trained model to continually changing target domains. Despite recent advancements in addressing CTTA, two critical issues remain: 1) Fixed thresholds for pseudo-labeling in existing methodologies lead to low-quality pseudo-labels, as model confidence varies across categories and domains; 2) Stochastic parameter restoration methods for mitigating catastrophic forgetting fail to preserve critical information effectively, due to their intrinsic randomness. To tackle these challenges for detection models in CTTA scenarios, we present AMROD, featuring three core components. Firstly, the object-level contrastive learning module extracts object-level features for contrastive learning to refine the feature representation in the target domain. Secondly, the adaptive monitoring module dynamically skips unnecessary adaptation and updates the category-specific threshold based on predicted confidence scores to enable efficiency and improve the quality of pseudo-labels. Lastly, the adaptive randomized restoration mechanism selectively reset inactive parameters with higher possibilities, ensuring the retention of essential knowledge. We demonstrate the effectiveness of AMROD on four CTTA object detection tasks, where AMROD outperforms existing methods, especially achieving a 3.2 mAP improvement and a 20% increase in efficiency on the Cityscapes-to-Cityscapes-C CTTA task. The code of this work is available at https://github.com/ShileiCao/AMROD . Published: 2026-05-29T15:19:57+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.786 (must_read)

Real-world application models are commonly deployed in dynamic environments, where the target domain distribution undergoes temporal changes. Continual Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) has recently emerged as a promising technique to gradually adapt a source-trained model to continually changing target domains. Despite recent advancements in addressing CTTA, two critical issues remain: 1) Fixed thresholds for pseudo-labeling in existing methodologies lead to low-quality pseudo-labels, as model confidence varies across categories and domains; 2) Stochastic parameter restoration methods for mitigating catastrophic forgetting fail to preserve critical information effectively, due to their intrinsic randomness. To tackle these challenges for detection models in CTTA scenarios, we present AMROD, featuring three core components. Firstly, the object-level contrastive learning module extracts object-level features for contrastive learning to refine the feature representation in the target domain. Secondly, the adaptive monitoring module dynamically skips unnecessary adaptation and updates the category-specific threshold based on predicted confidence scores to enable efficiency and improve the quality of pseudo-labels. Lastly, the adaptive randomized restoration mechanism selectively reset inactive parameters with higher possibilities, ensuring the retention of essential knowledge. We demonstrate the effectiveness of AMROD on four CTTA object detection tasks, where AMROD outperforms existing methods, especially achieving a 3.2 mAP improvement and a 20% increase in efficiency on the Cityscapes-to-Cityscapes-C CTTA task. The code of this work is available at https://github.com/ShileiCao/AMROD .

item.title: Are Deep Learning Models Trained on Synthetic Data Actually Reliable in InSAR? A Self-Supervised Learning Approach for InSAR Phase Denoising
item.dc:creator: Kang Liang
Zhong Lu
Jinwoo Kim
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
item.description: Deep learning is revolutionizing the way InSAR data are processed. Supervised deep learning models trained on synthetic datasets have demonstrated strong performance according to conventional evaluation metrics. However, in practice, many supervised InSAR phase denoising filters tend to oversmooth fringe patterns and generate ghost fringes in decorrelated regions, raising concerns about the reliability of models trained solely on synthetic data in real-world applications. In this work, we introduce Noise2Fringe, an unsupervised learning framework that enables the direct training of denoising filters on noisy interferograms, eliminating the need for clean references, while achieving performance comparable to that of supervised models. This framework allows a fair comparison between models trained exclusively on real versus synthetic data, independent of the training scheme. On synthetic benchmarks, models trained on real data appear to underperform compared to those trained on synthetic data. However, in real-world interferograms, the opposite holds: synthetic-trained models oversmooth and generate ghost fringes, whereas real-trained models avoid these artifacts. These findings reveal (i) a much larger gap between synthetic and real data than previously assumed, (ii) an overestimation of the effectiveness of synthetic-trained models, and (iii) deficiencies in current evaluation methods that rely heavily on simulated benchmarks. We further find that incorporating auxiliary information, such as the Amplitude Dispersion Index, into the input can enhance denoising performance. Overall, this study demonstrates that models trained solely on synthetic data may be far less reliable in real-world applications than suggested by their benchmark results, and emphasizes that generating more realistic training datasets should take precedence over designing increasingly complex model architectures. Published: 2026-05-28T22:28:55+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing Score: 0.786 (must_read)

Relevance: 0.786 (must_read)

Deep learning is revolutionizing the way InSAR data are processed. Supervised deep learning models trained on synthetic datasets have demonstrated strong performance according to conventional evaluation metrics. However, in practice, many supervised InSAR phase denoising filters tend to oversmooth fringe patterns and generate ghost fringes in decorrelated regions, raising concerns about the reliability of models trained solely on synthetic data in real-world applications. In this work, we introduce Noise2Fringe, an unsupervised learning framework that enables the direct training of denoising filters on noisy interferograms, eliminating the need for clean references, while achieving performance comparable to that of supervised models. This framework allows a fair comparison between models trained exclusively on real versus synthetic data, independent of the training scheme. On synthetic benchmarks, models trained on real data appear to underperform compared to those trained on synthetic data. However, in real-world interferograms, the opposite holds: synthetic-trained models oversmooth and generate ghost fringes, whereas real-trained models avoid these artifacts. These findings reveal (i) a much larger gap between synthetic and real data than previously assumed, (ii) an overestimation of the effectiveness of synthetic-trained models, and (iii) deficiencies in current evaluation methods that rely heavily on simulated benchmarks. We further find that incorporating auxiliary information, such as the Amplitude Dispersion Index, into the input can enhance denoising performance. Overall, this study demonstrates that models trained solely on synthetic data may be far less reliable in real-world applications than suggested by their benchmark results, and emphasizes that generating more realistic training datasets should take precedence over designing increasingly complex model architectures.

item.title: DGPT: Domain-guided prompt tuning for improved generalization in vision-language models
item.dc:creator: Keyang Cheng
Haoming Hou
Liutao Wei
Hao Wan
Xin Guan
Akira David Diaz Campaña
item.description: Prompt tuning, which fine-tunes learnable prompt vectors using limited few-shot data, has emerged as an effective strategy for adapting CLIP to downstream tasks. However, existing methods predominantly focus on optimizing for specific tasks or datasets, causing significant overfitting to the data distribution and thus reducing performance in unseen domains or novel categories. To overcome these limitations, we propose a Domain-Guided Prompt Tuning (DGPT) framework. DGPT employs a dual approach designed to mitigate overfitting while retaining robust generalization capabilities. Specifically, DGPT constructs a learnable base-domain statistics space as a lightweight domain-guided prior, which facilitates synthesizing domain-aware correction signals for unseen domains via weighted combinations. Moreover, DGPT incorporates a cross-contrastive learning mechanism to constrain alignment between a modality branch with added prompts and a modality branch with frozen parameters, effectively reducing biases in downstream task adaptation. Extensive evaluations are conducted across three distinct downstream tasks—base-to-novel generalization, cross-dataset transfer, and domain generalization—spanning 11 benchmark datasets, together with additional robustness evaluation under blur, low-light, and adverse-weather degradations. Experimental results demonstrate that DGPT achieves consistent improvements over strong prompt tuning baselines, with gains of 0.38%, 0.25%, and 0.72% on the three main evaluation settings, respectively, indicating its effectiveness for few-shot classification under domain-shifted conditions. Published: 2026-05-28T15:05:07+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.784 (must_read)

Extensive evaluations are conducted across three distinct downstream tasks—base-to-novel generalization, cross-dataset transfer, and domain generalization—spanning 11 benchmark datasets, together with additional robustness evaluation under blur, low-light, and adverse-weather degradations. Experimental results demonstrate that DGPT achieves consistent improvements over strong prompt tuning baselines, with gains of 0.38%, 0.25%, and 0.72% on the three main evaluation settings, respectively, indicating its effectiveness for few-shot classification under domain-shifted conditions.

item.title: MGA-CLIP: A Multigranularity Attribution Framework for Cross-Modal Explainability in CLIP
item.dc:creator: Xiaotian Cheng
Tianyi Zhou
Siwen Yin
Hao Zhang
Wei Xiong
Weiping Ding
item.prism:publicationName: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
item.description: The contrastive language–image pretraining (CLIP) model has demonstrated remarkable performance in multimodal tasks, but the interpretability of its similarity-based cross-modal alignment mechanism has attracted considerable attention. However, existing visual explanation methods typically rely on single-gradient information, which is easily affected by noise and activation saturation, resulting in unstable and rough interpretations. To address this issue, we propose a multigranularity attribution framework for CLIP (MGA-CLIP) that provides a systematic analysis of the internal learning behaviors of deep multimodal neural networks. At the coarse-grained level, the method adopts the concept of integrated gradients (IGs) to construct an integration path from a baseline to the input, accumulating path gradients to obtain a stable gradient tensor, which is then used to weight the feature space for a robust global interpretation. At the fine-grained level, the method leverages the model’s cross-modal alignment characteristics and its internal attention dependencies to quantify the semantic relevance between visual patches and text embeddings, thereby capturing localized embedding interactions. Unlike previous works that treat global and local cues independently, our framework establishes an explicit linkage between channel-level global attributions and patch-level semantic reasoning, enabling consistent explanations across different granularity levels. By fusing the coarse- and fine-grained results, MGA-CLIP generates semantically consistent and cross-modally aligned explanations. Moreover, we employ the proposed attribution framework in a text-based adversarial patch experiment, demonstrating its strong capability to reveal the internal reasoning behavior of the CLIP and to provide insightful interpretability References for analyzing multimodal alignment mechanisms. Extensive experiments show that MGA-CLIP significantly outperforms existing methods in attribution stabil... Published: 2026-05-29T19:56:42+00:00 Venue: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems Score: 0.784 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Xiaotian Cheng; Tianyi Zhou; Siwen Yin; Hao Zhang; Wei Xiong; Weiping Ding

Journal: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems

DOI: 10.1109/tnnls.2026.3696329

Relevance: 0.784 (must_read)

The contrastive language–image pretraining (CLIP) model has demonstrated remarkable performance in multimodal tasks, but the interpretability of its similarity-based cross-modal alignment mechanism has attracted considerable attention. However, existing visual explanation methods typically rely on single-gradient information, which is easily affected by noise and activation saturation, resulting in unstable and rough interpretations. To address this issue, we propose a multigranularity attribution framework for CLIP (MGA-CLIP) that provides a systematic analysis of the internal learning behaviors of deep multimodal neural networks. At the coarse-grained level, the method adopts the concept of integrated gradients (IGs) to construct an integration path from a baseline to the input, accumulating path gradients to obtain a stable gradient tensor, which is then used to weight the feature space for a robust global interpretation. At the fine-grained level, the method leverages the model’s cross-modal alignment characteristics and its internal attention dependencies to quantify the semantic relevance between visual patches and text embeddings, thereby capturing localized embedding interactions. Unlike previous works that treat global and local cues independently, our framework establishes an explicit linkage between channel-level global attributions and patch-level semantic reasoning, enabling consistent explanations across different granularity levels. By fusing the coarse- and fine-grained results, MGA-CLIP generates semantically consistent and cross-modally aligned explanations. Moreover, we employ the proposed attribution framework in a text-based adversarial patch experiment, demonstrating its strong capability to reveal the internal reasoning behavior of the CLIP and to provide insightful interpretability References for analyzing multimodal alignment mechanisms. Extensive experiments show that MGA-CLIP significantly outperforms existing methods in attribution stabil...

item.title: D3S-Net: A Dense SAR Ship Detection Network for Dynamic Denoising and Structural Sparsity Deinterference
item.dc:creator: Shun He
Ruirui Yuan
Zhiwei Yang
Jiaxue Liu
item.description: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) is crucial for ship detection in marine monitoring, due to its all-weather, all-time, and wide-area imaging capabilities.However, coherent speckle noise, coastal scattering interference, and dense ship distributions reduce detection accuracy. To solve these problems, this paper proposes D3S-Net, a dense SAR ship detection network based on frequency-domain dynamic denoising and sparse structural interference suppression. First, a frequency-domain dynamic denoising module (F3D) is designed to suppress speckle noise. By converting features to the frequency domain and combining an attention mechanism to adaptively adjust thresholds, the module achieves differentiated noise reduction while preserving target features. Then, a Mamba-based sparse interference suppression module (MBSIS) is constructed using the sparse distribution prior of targets. After capturing global semantics via the Mamba state-space selection mechanism, redundant interference features are removed through sparse constraints and mask generation. Finally, an IoU-aware mechanism is introduced into the detection head to form a comprehensive ranking metric combining classification confidence and localization accuracy, optimizing the dense target filtering strategy. Experiments on the SSDD+ and R-SDD SAR datasets show that mAP is increased by 2.3% and 2% compared with the baseline, verifying the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method in speckle noise and interference suppression. Published: 2026-05-28T07:06:24+00:00 Venue: Digital Signal Processing Score: 0.784 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shun He; Ruirui Yuan; Zhiwei Yang; Jiaxue Liu

Journal: Digital Signal Processing

DOI: 10.1016/j.dsp.2026.106295

Relevance: 0.784 (must_read)

Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) is crucial for ship detection in marine monitoring, due to its all-weather, all-time, and wide-area imaging capabilities.However, coherent speckle noise, coastal scattering interference, and dense ship distributions reduce detection accuracy. To solve these problems, this paper proposes D3S-Net, a dense SAR ship detection network based on frequency-domain dynamic denoising and sparse structural interference suppression. First, a frequency-domain dynamic denoising module (F3D) is designed to suppress speckle noise. By converting features to the frequency domain and combining an attention mechanism to adaptively adjust thresholds, the module achieves differentiated noise reduction while preserving target features. Then, a Mamba-based sparse interference suppression module (MBSIS) is constructed using the sparse distribution prior of targets. After capturing global semantics via the Mamba state-space selection mechanism, redundant interference features are removed through sparse constraints and mask generation. Finally, an IoU-aware mechanism is introduced into the detection head to form a comprehensive ranking metric combining classification confidence and localization accuracy, optimizing the dense target filtering strategy. Experiments on the SSDD+ and R-SDD SAR datasets show that mAP is increased by 2.3% and 2% compared with the baseline, verifying the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed method in speckle noise and interference suppression.
